title: Money Troubles

jokes.text: Let's go, Rieger. Heave the old tush this way.
jokes.setup: Louie addressing Alex in his typical crude manner
jokes.punchline: The antiquated slang 'old tush' delivered with Louie's characteristic lack of tact
jokes.explanation: Classic Louie character moment - crude but not particularly clever

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Listen here, you old tush!

jokes.text: Where's your friend Burns? / I want to give him a kiss.
jokes.setup: Louie asking about John's whereabouts
jokes.punchline: Sarcastic 'kiss' when he clearly has hostile intentions
jokes.explanation: Good character-driven sarcasm, economical delivery

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Where's John?

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: He's in the garage.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Thanks, pal. *blows sarcastic kiss* I'm gonna go kill him.

jokes.text: That's why you notice I never fraternize with you drivers. / I thought that was our choice.
jokes.setup: Louie claiming he chooses not to fraternize
jokes.punchline: Alex pointing out the drivers avoid Louie, not the other way around
jokes.explanation: Excellent comeback that deflates Louie's pretension with perfect timing and economy

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: I choose not to fraternize with the drivers.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Louie, the drivers avoid you. You don't avoid them.

jokes.text: Why hasn't the young couple invited me to dinner? / After all you've done for them.
jokes.setup: Louie wondering why he hasn't been invited to dinner
jokes.punchline: Alex's sarcastic 'after all you've done' highlighting Louie's terrible treatment of them
jokes.explanation: Good character interplay showing Alex's growing confidence in standing up to Louie

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Why haven't I been invited to dinner?

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: After all you've done for us?

jokes.text: I am so hungry I could eat a dog. / Not quite, huh, Latka?
jokes.setup: Latka trying to learn English idioms
jokes.punchline: Getting the idiom wrong in a disturbing way (dog instead of horse)
jokes.explanation: Classic Latka character comedy based on language barriers and cultural confusion

dialogue.speaker: Latka
dialogue.line: Alex, I want to learn the idioms. What is 'you can lead a horse to water'?

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Well, it means you can't force someone to do something they don't want to do.

dialogue.speaker: Latka
dialogue.line: Ah, I understand! You can lead a dog to water, but you cannot make him... drown.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: No, no, Latka...

jokes.text: Eat horse? Echhh! / You'd rather eat a dog?
jokes.setup: Latka being disgusted by eating a horse
jokes.punchline: Alex pointing out the logical inconsistency that Latka prefers eating dogs
jokes.explanation: Good absurdist logic highlighting cultural differences in food taboos

dialogue.speaker: Latka
dialogue.line: A horse?! I cannot eat a horse!

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Why not? You eat dog all the time in your country.

dialogue.speaker: Latka
dialogue.line: Yes, but a horse is a pet!

jokes.text: Please pass the salt. / No. Soon. Soon. / He likes salt.
jokes.setup: Latka repeatedly interrupting dinner lessons to ask for salt
jokes.punchline: His obsession with salt derailing the etiquette training
jokes.explanation: Sets up running gag of Latka's salt obsession

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Now Latka, when you're dining with someone important, you don't just grab the salt. You ask politely: 'Could you please pass the salt?'

dialogue.speaker: Latka
dialogue.line: Could you please pass the salt?

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Good! Now let's try the bread course. You take a small piece, butter it lightly...

dialogue.speaker: Latka
dialogue.line: Could you please pass the salt?

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Latka, we just had salt.

dialogue.speaker: Latka
dialogue.line: I know, but now I need more salt.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: This is hopeless.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Latka, proper etiquette requires restraint. You don't use that much salt.

dialogue.speaker: Latka
dialogue.line: In my country, we put salt on everything. Salt on eggs, salt on fish, salt on salt!

jokes.text: I'm so hungry I could eat a horse. / Echhh!
jokes.setup: John asking if Latka is hungry, Latka finally gets the idiom right
jokes.punchline: Still disgusted by the prospect despite saying it correctly
jokes.explanation: Effective callback that shows progression while maintaining character consistency

dialogue.speaker: John
dialogue.line: Are you hungry, Latka?

dialogue.speaker: Latka
dialogue.line: I could eat a horse.

dialogue.speaker: John
dialogue.line: Well, there's a deli around the corner.

dialogue.speaker: Latka
dialogue.line: No, no, no. I could eat a horse.

jokes.text: Burns, I got a pencil in my hand, and I'm writing down the words, 'Dinner... with... Burns.'
jokes.setup: Louie confronting John about dinner invitation
jokes.punchline: Melodramatic dictation of simple words as if it's momentous
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Louie's theatrical manipulation tactics

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: You invited me to dinner? Me? Louie De Palma?

dialogue.speaker: John
dialogue.line: Yeah, Louie. It's just dinner.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Just dinner. JUST dinner. Take a letter. Dear God. It has come to pass. The day I have waited for. Louie De Palma. Invited. To dinner. Period. New paragraph. I don't know what I did to deserve this honor. But I accept. Humbly. Sincerely. Louie.

jokes.text: All I wanted was an invitation. And I would say, 'Thanks, but no, because I make it a policy never to fraternize with the help.'
jokes.setup: Louie explaining he just wanted to be invited
jokes.punchline: Revealing he would have declined anyway, making his anger purely about ego
jokes.explanation: Excellent character revelation showing Louie's petty psychology - wants the validation of being asked even though he'd refuse

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: I just wanted to be invited!

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: But I would've turned you down anyway.

jokes.text: You hurt me, Burns.
jokes.setup: Louie's angry rant about not being invited
jokes.punchline: Sudden vulnerable admission delivered with unexpected sincerity
jokes.explanation: Effective tonal shift showing unexpected vulnerability beneath Louie's bluster

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: You know what really gets me? Nobody invites me to anything. I'm always left out. Nobody cares about Louie De Palma. I throw my money around, I'm nice to people, and what do I get? Nothing!

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: The truth is... I just want somebody to like me. Just once, I want to feel like I belong somewhere. Like I matter to someone.

jokes.text: I'd be delighted.
jokes.setup: John reluctantly inviting Louie to dinner
jokes.punchline: Louie's immediate, overly formal acceptance after his manipulative tantrum
jokes.explanation: Good payoff to manipulation sequence - shows Louie got exactly what he wanted

dialogue.speaker: John
dialogue.line: Louie, would you like to come to dinner?

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Me? You're inviting ME? After all these years of loyal service, you finally acknowledge my existence?

dialogue.speaker: John
dialogue.line: Louie...

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: No, no, it's fine. I understand. I'm just the dispatcher. Why would you want me around?

dialogue.speaker: John
dialogue.line: So is that a yes?

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Why, I would be absolutely delighted to accept your generous invitation. Thank you ever so much.

jokes.text: Gee, I can hardly wait for my first sunburn.
jokes.setup: Elaine kissing John's burned tongue to make it better
jokes.punchline: John sarcastically anticipating more 'medical treatment' from his wife
jokes.explanation: Decent observational humor about newlywed affection

dialogue.speaker: John
dialogue.line: Well, I'm looking forward to her treating my broken leg.

jokes.text: Brown!
jokes.setup: Being asked if his homemade wine is red or white
jokes.punchline: Cheerfully announcing an unappetizing third option
jokes.explanation: Perfect Latka moment - one word that's both unexpected and perfectly in character

dialogue.speaker: Latka
dialogue.line: Is neither! Is green!

jokes.text: What's wrong? / I think I stole his line. / I'm sorry, Latka.
jokes.setup: John accidentally using Latka's rehearsed 'horse' line
jokes.punchline: Awkward realization and apology for stealing his moment
jokes.explanation: Good cringe moment about social awkwardness and stepping on someone's prepared moment

dialogue.speaker: John
dialogue.line: I didn't know that was your line, Latka. I'm sorry.

dialogue.speaker: Latka
dialogue.line: You steal my moment! I practice this!

dialogue.speaker: Suzanne
dialogue.line: John, how could you? You know how hard he worked on that.

dialogue.speaker: John
dialogue.line: I didn't mean to. It just came out.

dialogue.speaker: Latka
dialogue.line: Is okay. But next time, you ask first, yes?

dialogue.speaker: John
dialogue.line: You're right. I'm really sorry, Latka.

jokes.text: Yeah, it goes equally as well with fish or meat or... waffles.
jokes.setup: Describing his unusual wine
jokes.punchline: The unexpected addition of 'waffles' as a food pairing
jokes.explanation: Classic Latka absurdism - adding an incongruous item to a normal list

dialogue.speaker: Latka
dialogue.line: This is a very special wine from my country. You pair it with waffles.

dialogue.speaker: Danny
dialogue.line: Waffles?

dialogue.speaker: Latka
dialogue.line: Yes, waffles. In my country, we know about wine and food pairing.

jokes.text: Oh, uh, does anyone know a really long toast? / Vootka! / That's it, huh?
jokes.setup: John asking for a long toast
jokes.punchline: Latka's enthusiastic one-word 'toast' followed by John's deflated reaction
jokes.explanation: Well-structured setup and payoff with good character specificity

dialogue.speaker: Latka
dialogue.line: TOAST!

dialogue.speaker: John
dialogue.line: ...that's it?

jokes.text: Could you please pass the salt? / I'm sorry. I swear it will never happen again. Never.
jokes.setup: Latka finally using his rehearsed polite dinner phrase
jokes.punchline: John's over-the-top apology as if it's a major transgression
jokes.explanation: Payoff to running gag, though John's reaction is overwrought

dialogue.speaker: Latka
dialogue.line: Please to pass the salt.

dialogue.speaker: John
dialogue.line: Oh my God, I'm so sorry! I should have offered it to you immediately. I feel terrible. Please forgive me. I don't know what I was thinking. You must think I'm the most inconsiderate person in the world. I'm deeply, deeply sorry.

jokes.text: You must give me your recipe, my dear. / It's just a salad, Latka. / You must give me recipe. / I think you'd better.
jokes.setup: Latka demanding the recipe for a simple salad
jokes.punchline: His insistence despite being told it's just lettuce and tomato, plus Alex's warning
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Latka's cultural confusion about American food

dialogue.speaker: Latka
dialogue.line: I must have the recipe! It is magnificent!

dialogue.speaker: Suzanne
dialogue.line: It's just lettuce and tomato.

dialogue.speaker: Latka
dialogue.line: No, no, there is more! There must be more! The way you cut it, the way you arrange it—I must know your secret!

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Latka, it's really just lettuce and tomato. There is no secret.

dialogue.speaker: Latka
dialogue.line: You are hiding something from me! I will not leave until you tell me everything!

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Trust me, you don't want to go down this road. Once you start asking Latka for recipes, he never stops.

jokes.text: Boy, I sure love these little tomatoes. I used to hate the little guys, but then, one day, boom, I love them. Just like that. I still hate big tomatoes.
jokes.setup: Alex making conversation about cherry tomatoes
jokes.punchline: The arbitrary distinction between loving small tomatoes but hating big ones
jokes.explanation: Mildly amusing observational humor but feels like filler conversation

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: You know what I don't understand? People who love cherry tomatoes but can't stand regular tomatoes. What's the difference? It's the same tomato, just smaller. It's like saying you love your nephew but hate your brother. It's arbitrary.

jokes.text: Look, I'm all out of tomato banter. Can we try something else?
jokes.setup: Alex's awkward small talk about tomatoes
jokes.punchline: Self-aware admission that his conversational topic has been exhausted
jokes.explanation: Clever meta-humor acknowledging the awkwardness of forced conversation

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Well, I guess we've covered everything there is to say about tomatoes.

jokes.text: You like cauliflower? I always hated cauliflower.
jokes.setup: Tense moment between the couple
jokes.punchline: Alex desperately returning to vegetable small talk
jokes.explanation: Good callback showing Alex's discomfort with conflict by returning to safe topic

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: So... how about those carrots?

jokes.text: Then you'll go out and save Bambi's mother.
jokes.setup: John defending his forestry career aspirations
jokes.punchline: Suzanne's sarcastic reduction of forestry to a Disney cartoon reference
jokes.explanation: Sharp put-down that undermines John's career with pop culture reference

dialogue.speaker: John
dialogue.line: Forestry is a noble profession. I'd be working outdoors, helping preserve our natural resources.

dialogue.speaker: Suzanne
dialogue.line: Oh sure, you'll be out there with Bambi and Thumper, frolicking through the forest.

jokes.text: Melmac. You see that? Ever since I married you, I'm too poor to even get mad good.
jokes.setup: John getting frustrated and trying to curse
jokes.punchline: Blaming poverty for his inability to swear effectively
jokes.explanation: Creative and original line connecting poverty to emotional expression in an unexpected way

dialogue.speaker: John
dialogue.line: Son of a... son of a... blast it!

dialogue.speaker: John
dialogue.line: I can't even curse properly. You know why? Poverty. When you're poor, you can't afford the good curse words.

jokes.text: Let's do this again sometime.
jokes.setup: Extremely uncomfortable dinner ending with couple fighting
jokes.punchline: Latka's cheerful social nicety despite the obvious disaster
jokes.explanation: Perfect Latka moment - his cultural inability to read social cues creates brilliant uncomfortable humor

dialogue.speaker: Latka
dialogue.line: Same time next week?

jokes.text: A little short, huh? / No, a little long.
jokes.setup: Elaine assuming Alex is short on money
jokes.punchline: Alex's correction revealing he has too much money
jokes.explanation: Clever wordplay misdirection that sets up the moral dilemma of the episode

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: You must be short on money.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: No, I'm short on time.

jokes.text: How much do they need? / $2,500. / I don't think we know them.
jokes.setup: Tony offering to help with money problems
jokes.punchline: Immediate reversal when he learns the amount
jokes.explanation: Excellent setup and immediate deflation - perfectly timed reversal of generous impulse

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: Don't worry, I got money problems too. But listen, I help my friends. How much you need?

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Twenty thousand dollars.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: Twenty thousand?! Get outta here! You're on your own, pal!

jokes.text: Well, I've been driving 16 hours straight, I've got impossible problems, and my marriage is over. / Well, in that case, you don't look so bad.
jokes.setup: John listing his terrible circumstances
jokes.punchline: Alex's matter-of-fact assessment that he looks good considering
jokes.explanation: Good supportive friend humor - finding the silver lining in disaster

dialogue.speaker: John
dialogue.line: I'm broke, I'm behind on rent, my car just broke down, and I haven't had a date in six months.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Well, you look good for it.

jokes.text: Sounds like a nice, quiet evening. / Straight to sleep. Do you hear what I'm saying, Alex?
jokes.setup: John describing going straight to sleep with his new wife
jokes.punchline: Alex completely missing the sexual implication
jokes.explanation: Awkward sexual humor played through Alex's obliviousness

dialogue.speaker: John
dialogue.line: I told her I was exhausted, so we just went straight to bed.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: That was smart. You need your rest before the big day tomorrow.

jokes.text: John, are you sure you want to talk about this because I for one, can live without it.
jokes.setup: John continuing to overshare about his sex life
jokes.punchline: Elaine's direct request to stop the TMI conversation
jokes.explanation: Realistic workplace boundary-setting in response to oversharing

dialogue.speaker: John
dialogue.line: You know, Elaine, last night with my girlfriend was just incredible. The way she—

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: John, please. I don't need to hear this.

dialogue.speaker: John
dialogue.line: But wait, there's more! You see, we were in the kitchen and—

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: John! Stop. I'm begging you. No more. I don't want to know about your sex life.

jokes.text: Just say the words. Be nice. / Just one thing. How do I get her to say it?
jokes.setup: Elaine giving marriage advice about saying 'I'm sorry'
jokes.punchline: John revealing he wants Suzanne to apologize, not him
jokes.explanation: Excellent misdirection that reveals John's actual mindset and subverts the advice moment

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: You have to learn to say 'I'm sorry.' It's the most important thing in a marriage.

dialogue.speaker: John
dialogue.line: You're right. I'll tell Suzanne she has to start saying it.

jokes.text: It's stuck.
jokes.setup: John trying to open the apartment door
jokes.punchline: Simple admission that the door won't open, implying physical comedy
jokes.explanation: Basic physical comedy setup, nothing particularly clever

dialogue.speaker: John
dialogue.line: The door won't open.

jokes.text: Gee, I always wanted one of those. A couch that converts to a high chair.
jokes.setup: Alex seeing their furniture situation
jokes.punchline: Sarcastic comment about their makeshift seating arrangement
jokes.explanation: Good observational humor about student poverty and improvised furniture

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Yeah, nothing says 'home sweet home' like a milk crate and two phone books. I'm one broken chair away from being a furniture store's clearance section.

jokes.text: I'm saying, do you want it in cash or a check?
jokes.setup: Alex's long speech about the problems with loaning money to friends
jokes.punchline: Cutting through all the concerns to offer practical payment options
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Alex's basic decency overriding his reservations

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Look, I understand all the concerns about loaning money to friends. It can strain the relationship, create awkward situations, and make things complicated. But here's what we can do—we can set up a simple payment plan, maybe some reasonable interest, and keep everything documented so there's no confusion down the road.

jokes.text: Alex, there's no need for you to go to the bathroom. I know how I feel. / Yes, there is. I know how I feel.
jokes.setup: Alex offering to leave so they can discuss privately
jokes.punchline: Alex insisting he needs to leave because he has strong feelings about it
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Alex's discomfort with being in the middle of their decision

dialogue.speaker: John
dialogue.line: No, no, stay. We can talk about this in front of you.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: No, I really should go. I have very strong feelings about this.

jokes.text: So that's what that silly smile is on your face every night. That's peace of mind. / You bet. / I sure would miss that smile.
jokes.setup: John explaining his contentment with their situation
jokes.punchline: Suzanne's sweet realization about John's nightly expression
jokes.explanation: Sweet character moment that shows their genuine affection beneath the stress

dialogue.speaker: John
dialogue.line: I'm perfectly content with the way things are between us.

dialogue.speaker: Suzanne
dialogue.line: You know, I just realized something. Every night when you come home, you have this look on your face...

dialogue.speaker: John
dialogue.line: What kind of look?

dialogue.speaker: Suzanne
dialogue.line: Like you're happy to see me.

jokes.text: Here goes. [coin flip moment]
jokes.setup: Deciding their educational futures on a coin flip
jokes.punchline: The absurdity and tension of the moment
jokes.explanation: Dramatic tension built around absurd solution to serious problem

dialogue.speaker: John Burns
dialogue.line: Heads I go to college, tails I become a cab driver.

dialogue.speaker: Louis De Palma
dialogue.line: That's a stupid way to decide your future.

dialogue.speaker: John Burns
dialogue.line: You got a better idea?

dialogue.speaker: Louis De Palma
dialogue.line: Yeah. Double or nothing.

jokes.text: Oh, John, it's okay. I'll just be a nurse a little later, that's all. / You don't understand. I lied. It was tails.
jokes.setup: Suzanne accepting the coin flip result gracefully
jokes.punchline: John's confession that he cheated on the supposedly fair coin flip
jokes.explanation: Excellent character revelation showing John's desperation and moral struggle

dialogue.speaker: Suzanne
dialogue.line: Well, that's fair. A deal's a deal.

dialogue.speaker: John
dialogue.line: Actually, I have to tell you something. That coin was two-headed.

jokes.text: Just another day in the life of Alex Rieger, super jerk.
jokes.setup: Alex helping his friends with their money problems
jokes.punchline: Self-deprecating comment about being a 'jerk' for being too generous
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Alex's self-awareness about his tendency to help others at his own expense

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: I guess I'm just a jerk for being too generous with my friends.

jokes.text: Just another day in the life of Alex Rieger, super jerk.
jokes.setup: After agreeing to lend the money despite his reservations
jokes.punchline: Self-deprecating assessment of his pushover nature
jokes.explanation: Character awareness of his own people-pleasing tendencies

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: I'm a pushover. I know I'm a pushover. I lend money to people I don't even like. Last week I lent twenty bucks to a guy who deliberately parks in my spot.

jokes.text: Boy, we're going to have tongues wagging that night. Huh, toots?
jokes.setup: Louie talking about the dinner invitation
jokes.punchline: His delusional interpretation of it as a romantic date
jokes.explanation: Classic Louie delusion about women being interested in him

dialogue.speaker: Louie De Palma
dialogue.line: A dinner invitation? That's a date! She wants me!

dialogue.speaker: Louie De Palma
dialogue.line: I'm telling you, when a woman invites you to dinner, she's interested. This is it. This is my big chance.

dialogue.speaker: Louie De Palma
dialogue.line: I'm gonna wear my good shirt, the one without the stains. Well, without the big stains.

jokes.text: it's a couple of late-night drinks, a little disco dancing, and then magic time.
jokes.setup: Louie's fantasy about the dinner
jokes.punchline: Escalating to completely unrealistic romantic scenarios ending in 'magic time'
jokes.explanation: Peak Louie delusion with euphemistic 'magic time' as the capper

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: So there I am, at this fancy restaurant, candlelight, soft music playing. She's looking at me like I'm the most interesting guy she's ever met.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: We finish dinner, we go back to my place. She says, 'Louie, you're so sophisticated, so cultured.' We're on the couch, the fireplace is going...

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Then she says, 'Take me away from all this, Louie. Take me somewhere magical.' So I snap my fingers and—POOF!—we're in Paris.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: We're dancing on the Eiffel Tower. She's in my arms, the city lights below us. She whispers, 'This is the most romantic moment of my life.'

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: I snap my fingers again—POOF!—now we're on a yacht in the Mediterranean. She's feeding me grapes. I'm a king, she's a goddess.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: And then she looks at me and says, 'Louie, take me to magic time.' And I say, 'Baby, it's magic time!'

jokes.text: Okay, if you don't want to ride a rocket to the stars, that's your business. / Thanks. / Of course, if I want to tell everyone you did, that's my business.
jokes.setup: Louie's grandiose romantic metaphor
jokes.punchline: His threat to lie about sexual conquest
jokes.explanation: Typical Louie sexual harassment disguised as banter, showing his pathetic psychology

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: You're like a beautiful symphony, and I'm the conductor. I'm gonna make sweet music with you all night long.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Louie, that's the worst line I've ever heard.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Yeah? Well, you better laugh at my jokes and pretend you had a good time, or I'm gonna tell all the guys at the garage that you did.

jokes.text: Okay, and if I want to tell everyone you couldn't, that's my business.
jokes.setup: Louie threatening to lie about their 'relationship'
jokes.punchline: Elaine's perfect comeback threatening to tell the truth about his inadequacy
jokes.explanation: Perfect comeback that turns Louie's threat back on him with devastating efficiency - exactly the kind of response that puts him in his place

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: You better be nice to me or I'll tell everyone we're having an affair.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: And I'll tell them it's a lie.

tmdb_overview: John and his new bride are in financial straits, and only Alex has the resources to come to the rescue.
insight: Taxi's weakest opener: 66.9 score, zero standout jokes despite strong character work.
